title: Psalms 113
subtitle: Praise the Name of the LORD

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “Who Is Like the LORD Our God?”

heb.paragraph: "Who is like (mî) the LORD our God, the One who sits enthroned on high?" (v.5). The first of the Egyptian Hallel psalms (113–118), sung at Passover. Three hallelujahs frame the psalm (vv.1, 1, 9). God’s name is praised from sunrise to sunset (v.3), above the nations and heavens (v.4). The incomparability question: who is like THIS God?

notes.note: "Praise the LORD. Praise the name of the LORD. From the rising of the sun to the place where it sets, the name of the LORD is to be praised." Temporal and spatial totality: all day, everywhere.
"The LORD is exalted over all the nations. Who is like the LORD our God, the One who sits enthroned on high?" The incomparability question expects one answer: no one.

refs.note: Mary's Magnificat draws heavily on this psalm's imagery of lifting the lowly.
Hannah's song similarly celebrates God raising the poor from the dust.
God choosing the poor to be rich in faith echoes the psalm's exaltation theme.

hist.context: Psalm 113 begins the Egyptian Hallel (Pss 113-118), sung at Passover and major festivals. The threefold 'praise the LORD' opening and 'from the rising of the sun to the place where it sets' (v.3) establish universal scope. God's transcendence ('enthroned on high') paradoxically enables condescension ('stoops down to look').

sections.header: Verses 6–9 — “He Raises the Poor from the Dust”

heb.paragraph: "He raises the poor (dal) from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap; he seats them with princes" (vv.7–8). The God who sits enthroned on high STOOPS to look down on heaven and earth (v.6). The stoop is the miracle: the highest bends to the lowest. He raises the poor to sit with princes. He settles the childless woman as a happy mother (v.9). Hannah’s song (1 Sam 2:8) and Mary’s Magnificat (Luke 1:52) echo this psalm.

notes.note: "He stoops down to look on the heavens and the earth. He raises the poor from the dust." The divine stoop: God bends BELOW heaven to reach the dust. The exalted one humbles himself to elevate the humbled.
"He settles the childless woman in her home as a happy mother of children." The barren woman’s joy: Hannah (1 Sam 1–2), Elizabeth (Luke 1), and every woman who waited. Hallelujah.

refs.note: Hannah's barrenness resolved and song of praise illustrates this psalm's pattern.
Elizabeth's conception in old age fulfills the psalm's barren-woman-becomes-mother theme.
Paul quotes Isaiah 54:1 about the barren woman rejoicing, which shares thematic similarity with this psalm's celebration of reversals.

hist.context: Raising the poor from dust and ash heap to seat them with princes (vv.7-8) celebrates divine reversal of social hierarchies. The barren woman becoming a 'happy mother of children' (v.9) addresses one of antiquity's greatest shames. These reversals became paradigmatic for Jewish and Christian understanding of salvation.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: Who is like the LORD our God, the One who sits enthroned on high?" The incomparability question expects one answer: no one…

rec.text: ""Praise the LORD. Praise the name of the LORD. From the rising of the sun to the place where it sets, the name of the LORD is to be praised." Temporal and spatial totality: all day, everywhere."

rows.label: Enthroned on High — 113:1–5
rows.text: "Who is like the LORD?" Sunrise to sunset. Above nations and heavens. Incomparable.

hebtext.note: "Who is like (mî) the LORD our God, the One who sits enthroned on high?" (v.5). The first of the Egyptian Hallel psalms (113–118), sung at Passover. Three hallelujahs frame the psalm (vv.1, 1, 9). God’s name is praised from sunrise to sunset (v.3), above the nations and heavens (v.4). The incomparability question: who is like THIS God?

"He raises the poor (dal) from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap; he seats them with princes" (vv.7–8). The God who sits enthroned on high STOOPS to look down on heaven and earth (v.6). The stoop is the miracle: the highest bends to the lowest. He raises the poor to sit with princes. He settles the childless woman as a happy mother (v.9). Hannah’s song (1 Sam 2:8) and Mary’s Magnificat (Luke 1:52) echo this psalm.

themes.note: Mercy peaks: the divine stoop from highest to lowest. Sovereignty: enthroned above all. Prophecy: Hannah’s song and Mary’s Magnificat echo this. First Egyptian Hallel psalm.

prayer_prompt: The LORD raises the poor from the dust and seats them with princes. Consider asking God about His reversals — what lowly things He's raising, what proud things He's humbling.
